title: Trading with the Crowd
summary: We formulate and solve a multi-player stochastic differential game between financial agents who seek to cost-efficiently liquidate their position in a risky asset in the presence of jointly aggregated transient price impact, along with taking into account a common general price predicting signal. The unique Nash-equilibrium strategies reveal how each agent's liquidation policy adjusts the predictive trading signal to the aggregated transient price impact induced by all other agents. This unfolds a quantitative relation between trading signals and the order flow in crowded markets. We also formulate and solve the corresponding mean field game in the limit of infinitely many agents. We prove that the equilibrium trading speed and the value function of an agent in the finite $N$-player game converges to the corresponding trading speed and value function in the mean field game at rate $O(N^{-2})$. In addition, we prove that the mean field optimal strategy provides an approximate Nash-equilibrium for the finite-player game.

Abstract
We formulate and solve a multi-player stochastic diÔ¨Äerential game between
Ô¨Ānancial agents who seek to cost-eÔ¨Éciently liquidate their position in a risky
asset in the presence of jointly aggregated transient price impact, along with
taking into account a common general price predicting signal. The unique
Nash-equilibrium strategies reveal how each agent‚Äôs liquidation policy adjusts
the predictive trading signal to the aggregated transient price impact induced
by all other agents. This unfolds a quantitative relation between trading sig-
nals and the order Ô¨āow in crowded markets. We also formulate and solve the
corresponding mean Ô¨Āeld game in the limit of inÔ¨Ānitely many agents. We prove
that the equilibrium trading speed and the value function of an agent in the
Ô¨ĀniteN-player game converges to the corresponding trading speed and value
function in the mean Ô¨Āeld game at rateO(N‚ąí2). In addition, we prove that the
mean Ô¨Āeld optimal strategy provides an approximate Nash-equilibrium for the
Ô¨Ānite-player game.

1 Introduction
The phenomenon ofcrowding in Ô¨Ānancial markets has gained an increasing attention
from both academics and Ô¨Ānancial institutions over the past couple of decades. It
is a subject of numerous research works studying both theoretical and empirical as-
pects of the topic, including [12, 33, 4, 2, 6, 5, 22] among others. Crowding is often
considered to be an explanation for sub-par performances of investments as well as
the development of systemic risk in Ô¨Ānancial markets. The presence of largely over-
lapping portfolios comes at the expense of portfolio managers‚Äô proÔ¨Āts, also in terms
of transaction costs, as aÔ¨Éne positions usually lead to similar trades.
The existing literature on crowding concentrates both on analytic models that
explain some aspects of crowded markets behaviour and on data driven statistical
models. In the Ô¨Ārst class, Cont and Bouchaud [12] proposed a simple mathematical
model in which the communication structure between agents gives rise to heavy tailed
distribution for stock returns. This established a theoretical connection between
crowding and stock markets shortfall. The aforementioned portfolios overlap was
shown to be a considerable factor in the August 2007 Quant Meltdown. Cont and
Wagalath [13] proposed a simple multi-period model of price impact from trading
in a market with multiple assets. Their model illustrated how feedback eÔ¨Äects due
to distressed selling and short selling lead to endogenous correlations between asset
classes and it provided a quantitative framework to evaluate strategy crowding as a
risk factor (see also [14]).
Within the class of statistical models, Khandani and Lo [22] used simulated re-
turns of overlapping equity portfolios and showed that combined eÔ¨Äects of portfolio
deleveraging followed by a temporary withdrawal of market-making risk capital was
one of the main drivers of the 2007 Quant Meltdown. Caccioli et al. [6, 5] developed
a mathematical model for a network of diÔ¨Äerent banks holding overlapping portfolios.
They investigated the circumstances under which systemic instabilities may occur as
a result of various parameters, such as market crowding and price impact. Volpati
et al. [33] measured signiÔ¨Ācant levels of crowding in U.S. equity markets for momen-
tum signals as well as for Fama-French factors signals. In [28] an index reconstruction
methodology was developed in order to measure the crowding eÔ¨Äect on Russell indexes
around reconstitutions events.
A new approach that connects optimal execution of multiple agents to crowding
phenomena was proposed by Cardaliaguet and Lehalle [7]. More speciÔ¨Ācally, a mean
Ô¨Āeld game with an inÔ¨Ānite number of agents, where each agent executes a large order
on the same risky asset was considered. In their model, the aggregated permanent
price impact created by all players‚Äô transactions is modeled as an exogenous process,
which satisÔ¨Āes a consistency condition, while temporary price impact inÔ¨āuences each
agent individually. The solution of the game showed qualitatively that in an inÔ¨Ānite
player setting, the optimal trading speed is deterministic and that it is optimal to
2
follow the crowd but not too fast, as this could create additional trading costs.
In this work we further extend and develop the model proposed in [7] in order to
reveal new properties and insights on crowding that arise in optimal execution frame-
work. More precisely, we formulate and solve a multi-player stochastic diÔ¨Äerential
game between traders who execute large orders on one risky asset in the presence of
individual temporary price impact and jointly aggregated transient price impact. We
also assume that traders are observing a common exogenous price predicting signal.
The unique Nash-equilibrium strategies show how each agent‚Äôs liquidation strategy
adjusts the predictive trading signal for the accumulated transient price distortion
induced by all other agents‚Äô price impact. This unfolds a qualitative relation between
trading signals and the agents‚Äô aggregated order Ô¨āow in crowded markets. We refer
to Section 2 for the model setup and to Theorem 2.13 for the solution of the game.
One can observe from the explicit solution to the game in (2.25) that the aggregated
price distortionYuN
and the exogenous trading signalA are coupled. We therefore
conclude that the distortion is acting as an endogenous signal, created by the trading
strategies of allN players. In the inÔ¨Ānite player game we show that a similar coupling
holds (see (3.20)), however the price distortionňúY ňúőĹ is determined by an independent
equation (3.18), then it is plugged into the optimal trading speed of the individual
agent in (3.20), essentially acting as an exogenous signal. See Remarks 2.15 and 3.8
for further details. From the explicit solutions which were mentioned above, we no-
tice that both in the Ô¨Ānite-player and inÔ¨Ānite-player games the signal and the price
distortion can follow a similar direction at least for a while. This is demonstrated
in Ô¨Āgure 3, where a decreasing signal ampliÔ¨Āes the sell strategies and as a result, a
negative price distortion is created. Note that at some point in time the inventory
penalties force the agents to close their position and the price impact turns in an
opposite direction to the signal.
Here we summarise the main Ô¨Ānancial interpretation of our analysis:
(i) The consideration of a decaying price distortion in the Ô¨Ānite player game points
out that the cumulative order-Ô¨āow is in fact an endogenous signal, which is
observed and used by all traders in the game.
(ii) In various scenarios the order-Ô¨āow ampliÔ¨Āes the eÔ¨Äect of the exogenous price
predicting signal on the price process and on the traders‚Äô execution strategies at
equilibrium.
These interesting and surprising results cannot be derived by the Cardaliaguet and
Lehalle [7] model, as their model assumes that the permanent price impact is an
exogenous signal which satisÔ¨Āes a consistency condition. Indeed in the mean Ô¨Āeld
setting the contribution of each agent to this signal is inÔ¨Ānitesimal. Also Cardaliaguet
and Lehalle did not incorporate a price predicting exogenous signal in their model,
so their mean Ô¨Āeld optimal strategies are in fact deterministic and the order-Ô¨āow
ampliÔ¨Ācation eÔ¨Äect does not appear. In fact our results show that for a sell strategy
3
for example, a negative price predicting signal will motivate the traders to sell more
aggressively and their associated order-Ô¨āow will drop the price down even further
and create an endogenous signal in the same direction of the predictive signal. The
only reason that the system remains in equilibrium is due to the penalties of holding
inventory during and at the end of the trading period in the agents‚Äô performance
functional. See Section 5 for a qualitative analysis and illustrations of the results.
In this work we also formulate and solve the corresponding mean Ô¨Āeld game,
which describes the limit of inÔ¨Ānitely many agents (see Section 3 and Theorem 3.14).
We prove in Theorem 4.1 and Corollary 4.5 that the equilibrium trading speed and
value function of an agent in the Ô¨ĀniteN-player game converges to the corresponding
trading speed and value function of an agent in the mean Ô¨Āeld game at rateO(N‚ąí2).
Similar convergence result with relaxed assumptions but without the convergence
rate is given in and Theorem 4.7. This concludes that the aggregated order Ô¨āow,
which appears in the model as the cumulative transient price impact of all agents,
becomes an exogenous signal as the number of agents tends to inÔ¨Ānity. As a result, we
justify the a priori assumption of the exogenous price impact process in the simpliÔ¨Āed
model of Cardaliaguet and Lehalle. Finally we prove in Theorem 4.10 that the mean
Ô¨Āeld optimal strategy provides an approximate Nash-equilibrium for the Ô¨Ānite-player
game.
Some additional papers on optimal execution in multiplayer and inÔ¨Ānite player
gameshaveappearedrecently, withoutspeciÔ¨Ācreferencetocrowding. Wewilldescribe
these results in short and explain how this paper improves and extends them from
the mathematical point of view. We will start with papers which solve only the mean
Ô¨Āeld game (i.e., the inÔ¨Ānite player setting). Casgrain and Jaimungal [10, 11] studied
a mean Ô¨Āeld game in which each agent is executing a large order while creating both
temporary and permanent price impact. Their model generalises the basic model of
Cardaliaguet and Lehalle [7], as it assumes that traders may have diÔ¨Äering beliefs or
partial information on the price process. Huang et al. [21] also extended the mean
Ô¨Āeld model in [7] by introducing three classes of traders: a large agent, small high
frequency traders and noise traders. Finally, Fu et al. [20] extended the model to
liquidation under asymmetric information. Our results improve and extend these
papers, since we also solve the correspondingN-player game, which is known to be
less tractable. We further assume that the traders create in addition a transient price
impact that depends on the entire trading paths of all players. Lastly, we prove the
convergence of the optimal strategy of theN-player game to the mean Ô¨Āeld game
equilibrium.
A few recent papers deal with Ô¨Ānite player execution games for the case that
traders create transient price impact. Strehle [32] and Schied et al. [31] worked on
the problem in continuous time, while Schied and Zhang [30] and Luo and Schied [27]
studied the discrete time setting. These references describe a special case of our model
as they do not include a predictive signal, which is responsible for the randomness of
4
the equilibrium strategies, and they do not prove convergence results to a mean Ô¨Āeld
limit, as done in this paper.
Finite player price impact games and mean Ô¨Āeld games with permanent price
impact were studied by Evangelista and Thamsten [17], Drapeau et al. [16] and F√©ron
et al. [18], where a special attention is given to the last two papers, in which the
convergence of the Ô¨Ānite player equilibrium to the mean Ô¨Āeld equilibrium was derived.
We remark that the convergence results in [16] and [18] do not derive the convergence
rate, and of course their model did not include transient price impact which plays
a crucial role in our model. Moreover, in these papers the convergence proof uses
particularfeatures of themodel, whichdo notapplytothe transientpriceimpactcase.
In Section 6 we develop a method which not only provides the rate of convergence but
could be adapted to a more general class of models, which translate at equilibrium
to systems of FBSDEs.
Finally some additional convergence results on a Ô¨Ānite player game to a mean
Ô¨Āeld game were derived recently for liquidation games with self-exciting order Ô¨āow
by Fu et al. [19], which did not include a price predicting signal. These convergence
results are quite diÔ¨Äerent than the convergence results in this paper, as they derive
the convergence of a game with stochastic i.i.d. noise in the transient price impact
coeÔ¨Écients. In our setting the source of randomness is a common noise, which is the
exogenous signal. Moreover the convergence results in Fu et al. [19] do not derive the
convergence rate.
Structure of the paper: In Section 2 we deÔ¨Āne the Ô¨Ānite player game and derive
the Nash equilibrium. In Section 3 we present the corresponding mean Ô¨Āeld game and
derive its equilibrium. In Section 4 we present our convergence results and approx-
imated Nash equilibrium. Section 5 is dedicated to illustrations of the equilibrium
strategies. Finally, Section 6‚Äď12 are dedicated to the proofs of the main results.
